FT_IC1.evidence: Uses 10,588 participants from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, U.S. cohort

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures smartphone ownership (binary) and age of first smartphone acquisition (continuous) as specific smartphone variables; reported by caregivers

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects logistic regression models testing associations of smartphone ownership/acquisition with depression, obesity, insufficient sleep; F/t/OR statistics reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% CIs reported: smartphone ownership vs depression OR 1.31 (95% CI 1.05-1.63), obesity OR 1.40 (95% CI 1.20-1.63), insufficient sleep OR 1.62 (95% CI 1.46-1.79); age of acquisition OR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02-1.16) for obesity

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: three mixed-effects logistic regression analyses (Analysis 1-3) testing associations of smartphone ownership and acquisition with health outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Pediatrics 2026, DOI 10.1542/peds.2025-072941; not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership is the independent variable (predictor), not dependent variable. Paper reports smartphone → non-SM outcome associations (depression, obesity, sleep). FT-EC7 exclusion does not apply.
edge_cases_applied: E13 - smartphone ownership as exposure with non-SM outcome
E1 - smartphone with own coefficient reported in logistic models
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Primary finding: smartphone ownership and younger age of acquisition associated with higher risk for depression, obesity, insufficient sleep in early adolescence.

FT_IC1.evidence: 11,875 children from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen use' as a lifestyle factor in mediation models but does not measure specific smartphone OR social media variables separately. 'Screen use' alone without phone/SM breakdown does not qualify per inclusion criteria. Generic screen time composite without phone/SM specificity = NOT_MET

FT_IC3.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_IC4.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_IC5.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_IC6.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; paper excluded via FT-IC2 failure (generic screen use without specific smartphone/SM measurement)
edge_cases_applied: E18 - generic 'screen use' without phone/SM specificity
notes: EXCLUSION: Does not meet FT-IC2. While the paper examines lifestyle factors (including screen use) as mediators of psychiatric associations, it does not measure or report specific smartphone or social media variables separately.

FT_IC1.evidence: 10,075 youth from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD), ages 9-10 at baseline

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract reports 'self-reported screen time' as a predictor of PLE trajectories but does not measure specific smartphone OR social media variables. Generic 'screen time' without phone/SM specificity does not qualify per inclusion criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_IC4.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure; no PDF available

FT_IC5.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_IC6.evidence: Not evaluated due to FT-IC2 failure

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; paper excluded via FT-IC2 failure (generic screen time without specific smartphone/SM measurement)
edge_cases_applied: E18 - generic 'screen time' without phone/SM device specificity
notes: EXCLUSION: Does not meet FT-IC2. Paper uses generic 'screen time' as a lifestyle predictor without specifying smartphone or social media separately. No PDF available to verify detail.
